9. Exponential Families

Motivating question: efficient estimators seem nice. For what families do they exist? From last lecture, we know that must have a unique stationary point if an efficient estimator exists.

Proposition 9.1. If an efficient estimator exists for a family satisfying regularity conditions, the family must be an exponential family with natural statistic .

When exists, solving for the likeihood in Equation 8.12 by integrating yields

(9.1)

Definition 9.1 (Single-Parameter Exponential Family, OPEF). For some containing an open, nonempty set, a parameterized family of distributions over the alphabet is a single-parameter exponential family if it can be expressed in the form

(9.2)

for some choice of functions (natural parameter), (natural statistic), and (log base function).

Note that is not specified because it serves as a normalization constant determined by and . is called the partition function and is called the log-partition function.

Remarks:

  • The support of cannot depend on . For example for is not an exponential family.
  • Such a specification is not unique as you can apply affine transformations to and get a new (Example 9.1).

Examples:

  • Normal distribution.
  • Bernoulli distribution has pmf for . More general binomial and multinomial distributions.
  • A Markov chain . Distributions that have this structure are exponential (Section 9.8)

Additional definitions

    (9.3)

    so that .

    Any exponential family is canonical wrt to some . Just let and is implicit by normalization ( need not be injective, so it may not be identifiable, defined below).

  • The natural parameter space of a canonical exponential family is the collection of all possible such that the corresponding form of the distribution can be normalized: . is a convex set because is convex (proved later via in Proposition 9.2). Hence for any

Implications:

  • The mean of the score function is zero (recall this happens iff you can exchange differentation and integration from intepreting the regularity conditions).
  • is nonnegative since the variance is nonnegative. Hence is nondecreasing.

9.2. Constructions and Interpretations

9.2.1. Geometric Mean

Define the weighted geometric mean of two strictly positive probability distributions as

(9.13)

This describes a canonical exponential family parameterized in . In fact every canonical exponential family can be written as family constructed via weighted geometric mean of two suitably chosen distributions (see Theorem 9.1 from lecture for more information).

9.2.2. Tilting Distributions

Given a base distribution , define the tilted distribution

(9.14)

Thi sdescribes a canonical exponential family. Again, in fact any canonical exponential family having a finite alphabet can be expressed as a tilted distribution.

9.3. Connection to Efficient Estimators*

Two theorems

9.4. Multi-Parameter EFs

Definition 9.2. For some containing an open, nonempty set, a parameterized family of distributions is a -parameter exponential family with natural parameter , natural statistic , and log base function if each member of the family is of the form:

(9.15)

A canonical multi-parameter exponential family has and .

We can again get properties of the log-partition function (9.5.2).

9.5. Categorizing and Reducing EFs

9.5.1. Identifiable Families

We only care about distributions where is associated with a unique value of .

Definition 9.3 (Identifiability). The parameter is not identifiable from in a family if and only if there exists with such that .
Example

Theorem 9.1 (Identifiability of Natural Parameters in Exponential Families). The parameter of a canonical exponential family is identifiable if and only if the natural statistics satisfy no affine constraint, i.e.

(9.16)

holds only if

For general exponential families, we need identifiable and invertible on .

9.5.2. Minimal Families

Definition 9.4 (Minimal Exponential Family). An identifiable exponential family with natural parameters is minimal when the natural parameters satisfy no affine constraint, i.e.

(9.17)

Affine constraint implies reducibility:

Theorem 9.2 (Reducibility of Exponential Families). An exponential family of dimension that is not minimal can be equivalently expressed as one of dimension .

Think it’s just some linear algebra.

9.5.3. Full-Rank and Curved Families

Minimal exponential constraints satisfy no affine constraints but they may still satisfy nonlinear ones. In that case the natural parameter space does not include an open subset of . See Example 9.15 and Example 9.16. One way to see this is to find a Jacobian with nonzero determinant.

Example 9.15 and 9.16
Definition 9.5 (Full-Rank Exponential Families). A minimal exponential family of dimsenion is full-rank if contains an open-nonempty set in .

Otherwise we call it a curved family: is a manifold in of dimension less than .

Extreme example of a full-rank family

9.6. Reparameterization*

When we specify an exponential family, we specify the collection via and and a particular parameterization via . If we don’t care about choice of , we can use the canonical one.

In generative modelling, the parameters are not identifiable, but this is not important.

9.7. EFs over Finite Alphabets

For a finite alphabet , it is possible to construct a canonical exponential family that includes all positive distributions. Let

(9.18)

where . Then an arbitrary distribution ovre is a member of the family corresponding to the following choice of :

This is effectively a weighted mean of elementary distributions .

For distributions over infinite alphabets, it is possible to show that under suitable constrains there exists a sequence of exponential families that are dense in the space of all suitably well-behaved distributions. We will revisit this after developing the notion of closeness of two distributions.

9.8. Markov Structure as an EF*

Consider a Markov chain where with a strictly positive joint distribution.

  1. It factorizes as

    (9.19)

    for some strictly positive and that have and parameters.

  2. We can construct an exponential family in in the following way:

(kinda trivial just hardcoding stuff). If , the exponential family describing an unconstrained joint distribution has parameters while for a Markov structured joint it has only parameter.

For probailistic graph models, the collection of all distributions that are MRFs with respect to the graph is an exponential family.

9.9. Local Families of Distributions*

In this section, we show that sufficiently well-behaved families of distributions are effectively “locally” exponential. Take a taylor series expansion about some .

9.10. Computation for Inference with EFs

When computing , the data only participates through natural statistics and the log base function . Once we compute these numbers we can throw away .

In fact, we don’t need to keep to carry out ML estimation:

For example, in a canonical exponential family,

(9.20)

is convex provided positive definite, and the second term is linear. Hence when is a convex set, we have a convex optimization problem which can be efficiently solved via gradient descent.

This behavior is not limited to ML estimation. We can throw away in general, and is a sufficient statistic.

In pset 3 problem 3, we show that a regularizer can turn ML estimation in to BLS estimation for an exponential family.
